subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass $m$ is attached to a spring (of spring constant $k$ ) and has a natural angular frequency $\omega_0$. An external force $F(t)$ proportional to $\cos \omega t\left(\omega \neq \omega_0\right)$ is applied to the oscillator. The time displacement of the oscillator will be proportional to
question: A particle of mass $m$ is attached to a spring (of spring constant $k$ ) and has a natural angular frequency $\omega_0$. An external force $F(t)$ proportional to $\cos \omega t\left(\omega \neq \omega_0\right)$ is applied to the oscillator. The time displacement of the oscillator will be proportional to
solution:
For forced oscillations, the displacement is given by
$$
\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{A} \sin (\omega \mathrm{t}+\phi) \text { with } \mathrm{A}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_0 / \mathrm{m}}{\omega_0^2-\omega^2}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Two bodies A and B of equal mass are suspended from two massless springs of spring constant $k_1$ and $k_2$, respectively. If the bodies oscillate vertically such that their amplitudes are equal, the ratio of the maximum velocity of $A$ to the maximum velocity of $B$ is
question: Two bodies A and B of equal mass are suspended from two massless springs of spring constant $k_1$ and $k_2$, respectively. If the bodies oscillate vertically such that their amplitudes are equal, the ratio of the maximum velocity of $A$ to the maximum velocity of $B$ is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_1=\mathrm{A}_1 \omega_1 \\ & \mathrm{~V}_2=\mathrm{A}_2 \omega_2 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_1=\mathrm{A}_2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}=\frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2}=\frac{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_1}{\mathrm{~m}}}}{\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{~K}_2}{\mathrm{~m}}}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{~V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{K}_1}{\mathrm{~K}_2}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The time period of oscillation of a simple pendulum of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> suspended from the roof of a vehicle, which moves without friction down an inclined plane of inclination <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>, is given by :
question: The time period of oscillation of a simple pendulum of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> suspended from the roof of a vehicle, which moves without friction down an inclined plane of inclination <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>, is given by :
solution:
Acceleration of vehicle down the incline will be a=gsinα
In the frame of the vehicle, we have to apply pseudo force. The Force diagram is shown in figure.
Component of mg along the inclined (i.e. mgsinα) will balance the pseudo force. Therefore, geff=gcosα
               

Hence,

T=2πLgeff=2πLgcosα.

subject: Physics
q: When a particle executes simple Harmonic motion, the nature of graph of velocity as function of displacement will be
question: When a particle executes simple Harmonic motion, the nature of graph of velocity as function of displacement will be
solution:
The displacement equation of a particle is given by 

x=Asinωt+ϕ⇒sinωt+ϕ=xA

The velocity equation is given by,

v=ωAcosωt+ϕ⇒cosωt+ϕ=vωA

Since, sin2θ+cos2θ=1

⇒x2A2+v2ω2A2=1

The above equation represents an ellipse.

subject: Physics
q: The equation of motion of a particle is given by $x = a \sin\left(50t + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$ cm. The particle will come to rest at time $t_1$ and it will have zero acceleration at time $t_2$. The $t_1$ and $t_2$ respectively are _______.
question: The equation of motion of a particle is given by $x = a \sin\left(50t + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$ cm. The particle will come to rest at time $t_1$ and it will have zero acceleration at time $t_2$. The $t_1$ and $t_2$ respectively are _______.
solution:
The equation of motion is given by $x = a \sin\left(50t + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$.

Velocity is the rate of change of displacement:
$v = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = 50a \cos\left(50t + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$

The particle comes to rest when $v = 0$. For the first time it comes to rest:
$\cos\left(50t_1 + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = 0$

$50t_1 + \dfrac{\pi}{3} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$

$50t_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi}{3} = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$

$t_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{300} \text{ s}$

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity:
$A = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = -2500a \sin\left(50t + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$

The particle has zero acceleration when $A = 0$. For the first time it has zero acceleration:
$\sin\left(50t_2 + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = 0$

$50t_2 + \dfrac{\pi}{3} = \pi$

$50t_2 = \pi - \dfrac{\pi}{3} = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

$t_2 = \dfrac{2\pi}{150} = \dfrac{\pi}{75} \text{ s}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\pi}{300} \, s, \dfrac{\pi}{75} \, s$

subject: Physics
q: A hoop and a solid cylinder of same mass and radius are made of a permanent magnetic material with their respective axes. But the magnetic moment of hoop is twice of solid cylinder. They are placed in a uniform magnetic field in such a manner that their magnetic moments make a small angle with the field. If the oscillation periods of hoop and cylinder are <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></msub></math> respectively, then:
question: A hoop and a solid cylinder of same mass and radius are made of a permanent magnetic material with their respective axes. But the magnetic moment of hoop is twice of solid cylinder. They are placed in a uniform magnetic field in such a manner that their magnetic moments make a small angle with the field. If the oscillation periods of hoop and cylinder are <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></msub></math> respectively, then:
solution:
Time period of any magnetic body place in an external magnetic field is:-

T=2πIMB

Thoop=2πmR2MB ...... (1) Ihoop=mR2

Tc=2πmR22M2B  ...... (2) ISolid cylinder=mR22

From (1) and (2)

Th=Tc

subject: Physics
q: In figure (A), mass 2m is fixed on mass m which is attached to two springs of spring constant k. In figure (B), mass m is attached to two spring of spring constant k and 2k. If mass m in (A) and (B) are displaced by distance x horizontally and then released, then time period T1 and T2 corresponding to (A) and (B) respectively follow the relation.
question: In figure (A), mass 2m is fixed on mass m which is attached to two springs of spring constant k. In figure (B), mass m is attached to two spring of spring constant k and 2k. If mass m in (A) and (B) are displaced by distance x horizontally and then released, then time period T1 and T2 corresponding to (A) and (B) respectively follow the relation.
solution:
The time period of spring block system is given by T=2πmk.

In both the cases springs are connected in parallel,

In case 1: keq=2k and total mass is 3m. Therefore,

 T1=2π3mkeq=2π3m2k

In case 2: keq=3k and total mass is m. Therefore,

T2=2πm3k

⇒T1T2=92=32

subject: Physics
q: If a simple harmonic motion is represented by $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{dt}^2}+\alpha \mathrm{x}=0$, its time period is
question: If a simple harmonic motion is represented by $\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{dt}^2}+\alpha \mathrm{x}=0$, its time period is
solution:
$\omega^2=\alpha$
$\omega=\sqrt{\alpha}$
$T=\frac{2 \pi}{\sqrt{\alpha}}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Knowing initial position $x_0$ and initial momentum $p_0$ is enough to determine the position and momentum at any time $t$ for a simple harmonic motion with a given angular frequency $\omega$.
Reason (R): The amplitude and phase can be expressed in terms of $x_0$ and $\mathrm{p}_0$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Knowing initial position $x_0$ and initial momentum $p_0$ is enough to determine the position and momentum at any time $t$ for a simple harmonic motion with a given angular frequency $\omega$.
Reason (R): The amplitude and phase can be expressed in terms of $x_0$ and $\mathrm{p}_0$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A)
Both $(\mathbf{A})$ and $(\mathbf{R})$ are true and $(\mathbf{R})$ is the correct explanation of $(\mathbf{A})$
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { If we express position } x(t)=A \sin (\omega t+\phi) \\ & \text { then } x_0=A \sin \phi \\ & v_0=A \omega \cos \phi \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \phi=\frac{\omega x_0}{v_0} \\ & A=\sqrt{x_0^2+\frac{v_0^2}{\omega^2}}\end{aligned}$
Hence both position and linear momentum of a particle can be expressed as a function of time if we know initial momentum and position

subject: Physics
q: The position, velocity and acceleration of a particle executing simple harmonic motion are found to have magnitudes of $4 \mathrm{~m}, 2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and $16 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ at a certain instant. The amplitude of the motion is $\sqrt{x}, \mathrm{~m}$ where $x$ is ________
question: The position, velocity and acceleration of a particle executing simple harmonic motion are found to have magnitudes of $4 \mathrm{~m}, 2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ and $16 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ at a certain instant. The amplitude of the motion is $\sqrt{x}, \mathrm{~m}$ where $x$ is ________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & x=4 \mathrm{~m}, V=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{a}=16 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 \\ & |\mathrm{a}|=\omega^2 \mathrm{x} \\ & \Rightarrow 16=\omega^2(4) \\ & \omega=2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{v}=\omega \sqrt{\mathrm{A}^2-\mathrm{x}^2} \\ & A=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{v}^2}{\omega^2}+\mathrm{x}^2} \Rightarrow \mathrm{A}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{4}+16} \\ & A=\sqrt{17} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

q: The function of time representing a simple harmonic motion with a period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi>ω</mi></mfrac></math> is :
question: The function of time representing a simple harmonic motion with a period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi>ω</mi></mfrac></math> is :
solution:
Time period T=2πω'

πω=2πω'

ω'=2ω→ Angular frequency of SHM

Option (3)

sin2ωt=122sin2ωt=121-cos2ωt

Angular frequency of 12-12cos2ωt is 2ω

Option (4)

Angular frequency of SHM

3cosπ4-2ωt is 2ω.

So option (3) & (4) both have angular frequency 2ω but option (4) is direct answer.

q: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is connected to an elastic spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>640</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and oscillates in a damping medium of damping constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>kg</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . The system dissipates its energy gradually. The time taken for its mechanical energy of vibration to drop to half of its initial value, is closest to-
question: A block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is connected to an elastic spring of spring constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>640</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and oscillates in a damping medium of damping constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi>kg</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . solution:
Given,

 E=Eo2 
⇒A=Ao2
Amplitude of damped oscillation=A=Aoe-bt2m

⇒Ao2=Aoe-bt2m 

2=ebt2m 

ln⁡2=bt2m=10-22×0.1t=0.1t2

t2=10ln⁡2=10×12×0.693

t=10×0.693=6.93

t≈7 s.

subject: Physics
q:
For a body executing S.H.M. :
(a) Potential energy is always equal to its K.E.
(b) Average potential and kinetic energy over any given time interval are always equal.
(c) Sum of the kinetic and potential energy at any point of time is constant.
(d) Average K.E. in one time period is equal to average potential energy in one time period.
Choose the most appropriate option from the options given below :
question:
For a body executing S.H.M. :
(a) Potential energy is always equal to its K.E.
(b) Average potential and kinetic energy over any given time interval are always equal.
(c) Sum of the kinetic and potential energy at any point of time is constant.
(d) Average K.E. in one time period is equal to average potential energy in one time period.
Choose the most appropriate option from the options given below :
solution: The formula of kinetic energy in S.H.M. is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mfenced><msqrt><msup><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></msqrt></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is the amplitude of the S.H.M. the formula of potential energy is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>, so the net energy is defined as, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo><mo>=</mo><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> so the total energy is always constant. Since, energy changes from potential to kinetic and vice-versa the average <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo></math> in one time period is equal to the average <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo></math> in one time period.

subject: Physics
q:
Time period of a simple pendulum in a stationary lift is T. If the lift accelerates with g6 vertically upwards then the time period will be
(Where g= acceleration due to gravity)
question:
Time period of a simple pendulum in a stationary lift is T. If the lift accelerates with g6 vertically upwards then the time period will be
(Where g= acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
The time period of a simple pendulum is given by T=2πlg

In an upward accelerating frame the effective geff=g+a

⇒T2=2πlg+a=2πlg+g6

TT2=g+g6g=76

⇒T2=67T

subject: Physics
q:
The velocity of a particle executing SHM varies with displacement (x) as 4v2=50–x2. The time period of oscillations is x7 s. The value of x is ______.

[Take π=227]
question:
The velocity of a particle executing SHM varies with displacement (x) as 4v2=50–x2. The time period of oscillations is x7 s. The value of x is ______.

[Take π=227]
solution:
Given:

4v2=50-x2

⇒v=1250-x2

Comparing with standard equation of SHM, v=ωA2-x2, we get

ω=12

Now,

T=2πω=4π=887

Hence, required x=88.

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> executes a simple harmonic motion under a periodic force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>–</mo><mn>25</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The particle attains a maximum speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> during its oscillation. The amplitude of the motion is ______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> executes a simple harmonic motion under a periodic force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>(</mo><mo>–</mo><mn>25</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The particle attains a maximum speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> during its oscillation. The amplitude of the motion is ______<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Given, force F=(–25x) N

⇒0.25a=-25x

⇒a=-100x

Now comparing it with standard equation, a=-ω2x we get

ω=10.

Now, vmax=ωA

⇒ωA=4

⇒A=410=0.4 m

⇒A=40 cm.

subject: Physics
q: The time period of simple harmonic motion of mass M in the given figure is παM5K, where the value of α is _______.
question: The time period of simple harmonic motion of mass M in the given figure is παM5K, where the value of α is _______.
solution:
For series combination of the spring, equivalent spring constant is given by, 1keq=1k1+1k2.

Therefore, for combined system net spring constant keq=2k×k2k+k+k=5k3

Angular frequency of oscillation ω=keqm

⇒ω=5k3m

Period of oscillation τ=2πω=2π3m5k

=π12m5k

Therefore, α=12.

subject: Physics
q: The displacement of an object attached to a spring and executing simple harmonic motion is given by $x=2 \times 10^{-2} \cos \pi t$ metres. The time at which the maximum speed first occurs is
question: The displacement of an object attached to a spring and executing simple harmonic motion is given by $x=2 \times 10^{-2} \cos \pi t$ metres. The time at which the maximum speed first occurs is
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{x}=2 \times 10^{-2} \cos \pi t \\
& \mathrm{v}=-0.02 \pi \sin \pi t
\end{aligned}$
$\mathrm{v}$ is maximum at $\mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{2}=0.5 \mathrm{~sec}$

q: Two simple pendulums having lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$ with negligible string mass undergo angular displacements $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, from their mean positions, respectively. If the angular accelerations of both pendulums are same, then which expression is correct?
question: Two simple pendulums having lengths $l_1$ and $l_2$ with negligible string mass undergo angular displacements $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, from their mean positions, respectively. If the angular accelerations of both pendulums are same, then which expression is correct?
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \omega=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\ell}} \\ & \alpha=-\omega^2 \theta \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{~g}}{\ell_1} \theta_1=\frac{\mathrm{g}}{\ell_2} \theta_2 \\ & \Rightarrow \theta_1 \ell_2=\theta_2 \ell_2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: At a given point of time the value of displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is given as y=A cos30°. If amplitude is 40 cm and kinetic energy at that time is 200 J, the value of force constant 1.0×10x N m–1. The value of x is _____.
question: At a given point of time the value of displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is given as y=A cos30°. If amplitude is 40 cm and kinetic energy at that time is 200 J, the value of force constant 1.0×10x N m–1. The value of x is _____.
solution:
The formula to calculate the kinetic energy K of a particle executing SHM is given by

K=12kA2-y2   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the value of the unknown parameter.

K=12×k×A2-A2cos230°= 12×k×A2sin230°⇒200=12×1.0×10x×0.42×122= 2×10x100⇒102=10x-2⇒x-2=2⇒x=4

subject: Physics
q: As per given figures, two springs of spring constants K and 2 K are connected to mass m. If the period of oscillation in figure (a) is 3 s, then the period of oscillation in figure (b) will be x s. The value of x is _____ .
question: As per given figures, two springs of spring constants K and 2 K are connected to mass m. If the period of oscillation in figure (a) is 3 s, then the period of oscillation in figure (b) will be x s. The value of x is _____ .
solution:
In figure (a), the springs are connected in series. Therefore,

Keq=K1K2K1+K2=K×2KK+2K=2K3

Therefore, the time period

Ta=2πmKeq=2πm2K3=2π3m2K

In figure (b), the springs are connected in parallel. Therefore,

Keq=K1+K2=3K

Therefore, the time period

Tb=2πmKeq=2πm3K

Now,

TaTb=92⇒Tb=23Ta=2 s

Hence, x=2

q: A damped harmonic oscillator has a frequency of <math><mn>5</mn></math> oscillations per second. The amplitude drops to half its value for every 10 oscillations. The time it will take to drop to <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>1000</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> of the original amplitude is close to:
question: A damped harmonic oscillator has a frequency of <math><mn>5</mn></math> oscillations per second. The amplitude drops to half its value for every 10 oscillations. The time it will take to drop to <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>1000</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> of the original amplitude is close to:
solution:
f=5
So, T=15
Time taken for amplitude to drop to half: 10T=105=2 s
Now,
A01000=A0(2)t2
(2)t2=1000
t2log2=3
t=6log2≈20 s

q: Two light identical springs of spring constant k are attached horizontally at the two ends of a uniform horizontal rod AB of length l and mass m. The rod is pivoted at its center 'O' and can rotate freely in horizontal plane. The other ends of the two springs are fixed to rigid supports as shown in figure. The rod is gently pushed through a small angle and released. The frequency of resulting oscillation is:
question: Two light identical springs of spring constant k are attached horizontally at the two ends of a uniform horizontal rod AB of length l and mass m. The rod is pivoted at its center 'O' and can rotate freely in horizontal plane. The other ends of the two springs are fixed to rigid supports as shown in figure. The rod is gently pushed through a small angle and released. The frequency of resulting oscillation is:
solution:
Torque on the rod about O,



τ=kx.l2×2

I α​=k.l2.θ.l2×2

I α​=kl22.θ

∴ ω2=kl22Ml212=6kM

f=12π6kM

subject: Physics
q: In simple harmonic motion, the total mechanical energy of given system is $E$. If mass of oscillating particle $P$ is doubled then the new energy of the system for same amplitude is:
question: In simple harmonic motion, the total mechanical energy of given system is $E$. If mass of oscillating particle $P$ is doubled then the new energy of the system for same amplitude is:
solution:
$\text {T.E. }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kA}^2$
since A is same T.E. will be same.

question: A mass of 5 kg is connected to a spring. The potential energy curve of the simple harmonic motion executed by the system is shown in the figure. solution:
From the figure, A=2 m

12mω2A2=10
12mω2(2)2=10
mω2=5
5ω2=5
ω2=1
ω=1
T=2πω=2π
2πℓgp=2π
2π4gp=2π
gp=4m/s2

q: Assume that a tunnel is dug along a chord of the earth, at a perpendicular distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> from the earth's centre, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of the earth. The wall of the tunnel is frictionless. If a particle is released in this tunnel, it will execute a simple harmonic motion with a time period:
question: Assume that a tunnel is dug along a chord of the earth, at a perpendicular distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> from the earth's centre, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the radius of the earth. The wall of the tunnel is frictionless. If a particle is released in this tunnel, it will execute a simple harmonic motion with a time period:
options: Image is virtual, opposite side of convex mirror.
solution:
Force along the tunnel

F=-GMmrR3cosθ

F=-gmRx  GMR2=g, rcosθ=x

a=-gRx

ω2=gR

T=2πRg

subject: Physics
q: The general displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mo> </mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> be its time period. The slope of its potential energy (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi></math>) – time (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>) curve will be maximum when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>T</mi><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> is ______.
question: The general displacement of a simple harmonic oscillator is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mo> </mo><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> be its time period. The slope of its potential energy (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi></math>) – time (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math>) curve will be maximum when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>T</mi><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Given here, displacement x=A sin ωt

Now, potential energy of a simple harmonic oscillator is U=12mω2x2=12mω2A2sin2ωt,

So, slope of potential energy and time curve is dUdt=12mω3A22sin2ωt

Now, dUdtmax will be maximum when sin2ωt=1

 ⇒2ωt=π2⇒22πTt=π2⇒t=T8⇒β=8

subject: Physics
q: A particle executes simple harmonic motion. Its amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and time period is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The time it will take to travel from its position of maximum displacement to the point corresponding to half of its amplitude, is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>
question: A particle executes simple harmonic motion. Its amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and time period is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The time it will take to travel from its position of maximum displacement to the point corresponding to half of its amplitude, is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>
solution:
Given here, A=8 cm and T=6 s

The displacement equation in SHM, when amplitude is halved is Acos2πtT=A2

⇒2πtT=π3

Time taken by the particle is t= T6=66=1sec.

subject: Physics
q: An object of mass $0.2 \mathrm{~kg}$ executes simple harmonic motion along $\mathrm{x}$ axis with frequency of $\left(\frac{25}{\pi}\right) \mathrm{Hz}$. At the position $x=0.04 \mathrm{~m}$ the object has kinetic energy $0.5 \mathrm{~J}$ and potential energy $0.4 \mathrm{~J}$. The amplitude of oscillation is _____$\mathrm{cm}$.
question: An object of mass $0.2 \mathrm{~kg}$ executes simple harmonic motion along $\mathrm{x}$ axis with frequency of $\left(\frac{25}{\pi}\right) \mathrm{Hz}$. At the position $x=0.04 \mathrm{~m}$ the object has kinetic energy $0.5 \mathrm{~J}$ and potential energy $0.4 \mathrm{~J}$. The amplitude of oscillation is _____$\mathrm{cm}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Total energy }=\text { K.E. }+ \text { P.E. } \\ & \text { at } x=0.04 \mathrm{~m}, \text { T.E. }=0.5+0.4=0.9 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \begin{aligned} \text { T.E } & =1 \mathrm{~m} \omega^2 \mathrm{~A}^2=0.9 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 0.2\left(2 \pi \times \frac{25}{\pi}\right)^2 \times \mathrm{A}^2=0.9 \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{A} & =0.06 \mathrm{~m} \\ \mathrm{~A} & =6 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Starting from the origin, a body oscillates simple harmonically with a period of $2 \mathrm{~s}$. After what time will its kinetic energy be $75 \%$ of the total energy?
question: Starting from the origin, a body oscillates simple harmonically with a period of $2 \mathrm{~s}$. After what time will its kinetic energy be $75 \%$ of the total energy?
solution:
$\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{3}{4}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v_{\max }^2\right)$
$\mathrm{A}^2 \omega^2 \cos ^2 \omega \mathrm{t} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{4} \mathrm{~A}^2 \omega^2$
$\cos \omega \mathrm{t}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\omega t=\frac{\pi}{6} \Rightarrow t=\frac{1}{6}$ sec

The displacement of the particle when its speed is half of maximum speed is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: A particle executes S.H.M. with amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. The displacement of the particle when its speed is half of maximum speed is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mi>A</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
V=ωA2-x2   Vmax=Aω

Aω2=ωA2-x2

A24=A2-x2

x2=3 A24

x=32 A

subject: Physics
q: A pendulum bob has a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at its lowest position. The pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> long. The speed of bob, when the length makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the vertical will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
question: A pendulum bob has a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> at its lowest position. The pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> long. The speed of bob, when the length makes an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the vertical will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>.
solution:
Applying work energy theorem:

wg+wT=ΔK

-mgl1-cos60°=12mv2-12mu2

v2=u2-2gl1-cos60°

v2=9-2×10×0.512

v2=4

v=2 m s-1

Two simple harmonic motion, are represented by the equations

y1=10sin3πt+π3;  y2=5(sin3πt+3cos3πt)

Ratio of amplitude of y1 to y2=x:1. The value of x is
question:

Ratio of amplitude of y1 to y2=x:1. The value of x is

q: Two simple harmonic motions are represented by the equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>(</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The amplitude of the second motion is _____ times the amplitude in the first motion.
question: Two simple harmonic motions are represented by the equations <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>(</mo><mi>sin</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>.</mo></math> The amplitude of the second motion is _____ times the amplitude in the first motion.
solution:
A1=5

x2=52[sin2πt+cos2πt]

x2=10sin2πT+π4

A2=10

A2=2 A1
